title: Point/Vector: Type variants
analysis: The Type enum in crates/reify-types/src/ty.rs currently has 15 variants with #[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]. Display is manually implemented in the same file. Factory methods (length(), angle(), dimensionless_scalar()) follow a consistent pattern of constructing Type variants with preset fields. The only exhaustive match on Type is the Display impl itself — all other match sites in the codebase (cpsat.rs, compiler, etc.) use wildcard catch-all arms, so adding two new variants will only require updating ty.rs and the exports integration test. No content_hash impl exists for Type (it uses derived Hash), so no hash tag assignments needed. The new Point { n, quantity } and Vector { n, quantity } variants follow the same structural pattern as List(Box<Type>) and Set(Box<Type>) — a container with an inner type — but additionally carry a dimensionality parameter n:usize. Display will follow the format Point{n}<{quantity}> (e.g., Point3<Scalar[m]>) and Vector{n}<{quantity}> consistent with the existing angle-bracket convention for generic types.

steps.description: Write failing tests for Point variant Display: assert Point { n:3, quantity: Type::length() } displays as 'Point3<Scalar[m]>', Point { n:2, quantity: Type::Real } as 'Point2<Real>', Point { n:1, quantity: Type::Int } as 'Point1<Int>'
Add Point { n: usize, quantity: Box<Type> } variant to Type enum and add Display match arm: write!(f, 'Point{}<{}>', n, quantity)
Write failing tests for Vector variant Display: assert Vector { n:3, quantity: Type::length() } displays as 'Vector3<Scalar[m]>', Vector { n:2, quantity: Type::Real } as 'Vector2<Real>'
Add Vector { n: usize, quantity: Box<Type> } variant to Type enum and add Display match arm: write!(f, 'Vector{}<{}>', n, quantity)

Write failing tests for factory methods: assert Type::point2(Type::length()) == Type::Point { n:2, quantity: Box::new(Type::length()) } and Type::point3(Type::Real) == Type::Point { n:3, quantity: Box::new(Type::Real) }
Implement pub fn point2(quantity: Type) -> Self and pub fn point3(quantity: Type) -> Self factory methods on Type impl block

Write failing tests for factory methods: assert Type::vec2(Type::length()) == Type::Vector { n:2, quantity: Box::new(Type::length()) } and Type::vec3(Type::Real) == Type::Vector { n:3, quantity: Box::new(Type::Real) }
Implement pub fn vec2(quantity: Type) -> Self and pub fn vec3(quantity: Type) -> Self factory methods on Type impl block

Write tests for Eq and Hash: (a) Point3<Length> == Point3<Length>, (b) Point3<Length> != Point2<Length>, (c) Point3<Length> != Point3<Real>, (d) Point3<Length> != Vector3<Length>, (e) same assertions for Vector, (f) Hash consistency: equal types produce equal hashes via HashMap insert+lookup

Verify Eq/Hash tests pass (derived automatically — no code change expected). Also add test asserting Point and Vector are not numeric via is_numeric(), which should pass from the existing match wildcard-free is_numeric() using matches!() macro

Add Point and Vector variant construction to the all_m5_types_exported test in tests/exports.rs: construct Type::Point and Type::Vector variants, use Type::point3() and Type::vec3() factories, verify accessible from crate root
No impl change expected for exports — Type is already re-exported. Run full cargo test -p reify-types to verify everything passes end-to-end.

design_decisions.decision: Use Point{n}<{quantity}> Display format (e.g., Point3<Scalar[m]>) rather than domain-specific names like Point3<Length>
design_decisions.rationale: Consistent with existing generic type Display conventions (List<Int>, Set<String>, Field<Real, Real>). The inner type's Display is reused verbatim. If a friendlier 'Length' alias is desired, that would be a Type alias feature, not a Point-specific Display hack.

design_decisions.decision: Use Vector{n}<{quantity}> as Display format, not Vec{n}<...>
design_decisions.rationale: Avoids ambiguity with Rust's Vec<T> and is more explicit for the CAD/engineering domain. The variant name in the enum is Vector, so Display should match.

design_decisions.decision: Both Point and Vector store n:usize + quantity:Box<Type> with identical structure
design_decisions.rationale: Mathematically a Point and a Vector in an affine space are distinct types even though they share the same representation (n-tuple of quantities). Keeping them as separate variants enables the type checker to distinguish them, which is important for geometric correctness (you can subtract points to get a vector, but cannot add two points).

design_decisions.decision: Factory methods only for n=2 and n=3 (point2, point3, vec2, vec3)
design_decisions.rationale: Task specifies these four. The general variant Type::Point { n, quantity } supports arbitrary dimensions, but 2D and 3D are the common cases in CAD that deserve convenience constructors.

design_decisions.decision: Eq and Hash are derived, no custom implementation needed
design_decisions.rationale: Both new variants contain only usize (which derives Eq/Hash trivially) and Box<Type> (which delegates to Type's derived Eq/Hash). The derive macro handles this correctly with structural equality and deterministic hashing.

design_decisions.decision: Steps 9-10 test Eq/Hash even though it's derived
design_decisions.rationale: Explicit tests document the contract and catch regressions if someone later changes the derive to a manual implementation. Following the pattern of existing tests (e.g., type_field_variant tests equality).

reuse.what: Existing #[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)] on Type enum
reuse.how: New Point and Vector variants get Eq/Hash for free from the derive macro — no manual implementation needed.

reuse.what: Factory method pattern from Type::length(), Type::angle()
reuse.how: Follow identical pattern: pub fn point3(quantity: Type) -> Self { Type::Point { n: 3, quantity: Box::new(quantity) } }

reuse.what: Display arm pattern from List/Set/Field variants
reuse.how: Point/Vector Display follows the same write!(f, ...) pattern, using the inner type's Display trait via {} format.

reuse.what: Test patterns from existing type_field_variant and type_list_display tests
reuse.how: New tests follow the same assert_eq!(format!('{}', ty), expected) and equality assertion patterns.
Add Point/Vector variant construction under a new section in the existing test function, following the same let _var = reify_types::Type::Variant pattern.
